quiz_questions.question_text: What is the acceleration due to gravity near Earth's surface?

options.text: Approximately 9.8 m/s² downward
Approximately 9.8 m/s² upward
Exactly 10 m/s² upward
It depends on the mass of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: Near Earth's surface, all objects in free fall accelerate at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² directed downward, regardless of their mass (ignoring air resistance).

quiz_questions.question_text: In free fall (no air resistance), does a heavier object fall faster than a lighter one?

options.text: No — all objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass
Yes — heavier objects always fall faster
Yes — but only for short distances
It depends on the shape

quiz_questions.explanation: In the absence of air resistance, all objects experience the same gravitational acceleration g. Mass does not affect the rate of free fall — this was demonstrated by Galileo.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the vertical acceleration of a projectile at the top of its trajectory?

options.text: 9.8 m/s² downward — gravity never stops acting
Zero — the object momentarily stops
9.8 m/s² upward
It changes direction at the top

quiz_questions.explanation: At the peak, the vertical velocity is zero, but the acceleration is still g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Gravity acts continuously throughout the entire trajectory.

quiz_questions.question_text: In projectile motion, what is the horizontal acceleration (ignoring air resistance)?

options.text: Zero — no horizontal force acts on the projectile
9.8 m/s² in the direction of motion
It equals the vertical acceleration
It depends on the launch angle

quiz_questions.explanation: In ideal projectile motion, the only force is gravity (vertical). With no horizontal force, horizontal acceleration is zero, and horizontal velocity remains constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball is dropped from rest. After 2 seconds, what is its velocity? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 20 m/s downward
10 m/s downward
40 m/s downward
2 m/s downward

quiz_questions.explanation: v = u + gt = 0 + 10(2) = 20 m/s downward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball is dropped from rest. How far does it fall in 3 seconds? (Use g = 10 m/s²)
A projectile is launched horizontally from a cliff. What determines how long it stays in the air?

options.text: Only the height of the cliff and gravity — horizontal velocity does not affect time in the air
The horizontal velocity
The mass of the projectile
The wind speed

quiz_questions.explanation: For a horizontally launched projectile, the time of flight depends only on the vertical motion: t = √(2h/g). Horizontal velocity has no effect on the time to fall a given height.

quiz_questions.question_text: At what launch angle does a projectile achieve maximum range on level ground (no air resistance)?
quiz_questions.explanation: The range formula R = u²sin(2θ)/g is maximized when sin(2θ) = 1, giving 2θ = 90° or θ = 45°.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball is thrown straight up. What is its velocity at the highest point?

options.text: Zero — it momentarily stops before falling back down
9.8 m/s upward
9.8 m/s downward
It depends on the throw speed

quiz_questions.explanation: At the peak of vertical motion, the ball's velocity is instantaneously zero. It then begins falling back down under gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two identical balls are released simultaneously from the same height — one is dropped and the other is thrown horizontally. Which hits the ground first?

options.text: They hit at the same time — both have the same vertical motion
The dropped ball hits first
The horizontally thrown ball hits first
It depends on how fast the horizontal ball is thrown

quiz_questions.explanation: Both balls start with zero vertical velocity and fall the same height under the same gravitational acceleration. The horizontal velocity of the thrown ball does not affect its vertical motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes free fall?

options.text: Motion of an object under the sole influence of gravity, with no air resistance or other forces acting on it
Any downward motion of an object
Falling at a speed greater than terminal velocity
Motion where the object has no velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Free fall occurs when gravity is the only force acting on an object. In free fall, all objects accelerate at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² regardless of mass, shape, or size.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a projectile?

options.text: An object that has been launched or thrown and moves under the influence of gravity alone, following a curved path
Any object that is moving through the air
An object pushed by a constant force
A ball rolling on a surface

quiz_questions.explanation: A projectile is an object in flight with no propulsion — after launch, only gravity acts on it (ignoring air resistance). It follows a parabolic trajectory.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes trajectory?

options.text: The curved path that a projectile follows through space, which is a parabola for ideal projectile motion near Earth's surface
A straight line from start to finish
The total distance traveled by an object
The speed at which an object moves

quiz_questions.explanation: In ideal projectile motion (constant g, no air resistance), the trajectory is a parabola. It results from combining constant horizontal velocity with uniformly accelerated vertical motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes range in projectile motion?

options.text: The horizontal distance traveled by a projectile from its launch point to the point where it returns to the same height
The maximum height reached
The total path length of the projectile
The time the projectile spends in the air

quiz_questions.explanation: Range is the horizontal displacement of a projectile. On level ground, R = u²sin(2θ)/g, where u is the launch speed and θ is the launch angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the time of flight in projectile motion?

options.text: The total time from when the projectile is launched to when it returns to the launch height (or hits the ground)
The time to reach maximum height only
The time the projectile spends moving horizontally
The time it takes to stop

quiz_questions.explanation: Time of flight is the total duration of the projectile's journey. For level ground: T = 2u sinθ / g. It depends on the vertical component of the initial velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes maximum height in projectile motion?

options.text: The greatest vertical distance above the launch point reached by the projectile, occurring when the vertical velocity becomes zero
The horizontal distance at the midpoint
The height at which air resistance equals gravity
The launch height of the projectile

quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum height H = u²sin²θ / (2g) occurs when v_y = 0. At this point, only horizontal velocity remains (if launched at an angle).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes terminal velocity?

options.text: The constant maximum velocity reached by a falling object when the drag force equals the gravitational force, resulting in zero net force and zero acceleration
The velocity at which an object hits the ground
The initial velocity of a dropped object
The speed of light

quiz_questions.explanation: Terminal velocity occurs when air drag equals weight. At this point, net force = 0, acceleration = 0, and the object falls at constant speed. It depends on mass, shape, and air density.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the launch angle?

options.text: The angle between the initial velocity vector of a projectile and the horizontal
The angle of the ground relative to horizontal
The angle at which the projectile hits the ground
The angle between the projectile and the vertical

quiz_questions.explanation: Launch angle θ is measured from the horizontal to the initial velocity vector. It determines the split between horizontal and vertical velocity components: vₓ = u cosθ, v_y = u sinθ.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the vertical component of velocity?

options.text: The part of the velocity vector directed perpendicular to the ground, equal to v sinθ for a launch at angle θ, which changes due to gravity
The total speed of the projectile
The horizontal part of the velocity
The velocity at maximum height

quiz_questions.explanation: The vertical component v_y = u sinθ − gt changes with time due to gravitational acceleration. It determines time of flight and maximum height.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the horizontal component of velocity?

options.text: The part of the velocity vector directed parallel to the ground, equal to v cosθ for a launch at angle θ, which remains constant in ideal projectile motion
The velocity at maximum height
The velocity when the projectile lands
The rate of change of the vertical velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: The horizontal component vₓ = u cosθ remains constant throughout ideal projectile motion because there is no horizontal acceleration (no air resistance).

quiz_questions.question_text: A stone is dropped from a bridge 80 m above a river. How long does it take to hit the water? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 4 s — using s = ½gt²: 80 = ½(10)t² → t² = 16 → t = 4 s
8 s
2 s

quiz_questions.explanation: s = ½gt²: 80 = 5t² → t² = 16 → t = 4 s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball is thrown horizontally at 15 m/s from a cliff 45 m high. How far from the base of the cliff does it land? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 45 m — time to fall: t = √(2h/g) = √(90/10) = 3 s; horizontal distance = 15 × 3 = 45 m

quiz_questions.explanation: Time to fall: t = √(2 × 45/10) = √9 = 3 s. Horizontal distance = vₓ × t = 15 × 3 = 45 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cannonball is fired at 50 m/s at an angle of 30° above horizontal. What is its horizontal range? (Use g = 10 m/s²)
A ball is thrown upward at 30 m/s. A second ball is thrown upward at 30 m/s at a 60° angle. Which reaches a greater maximum height?

options.text: The vertically thrown ball — its entire velocity is vertical (v_y = 30 m/s), while the angled ball has v_y = 30 sin60° ≈ 26 m/s
The ball at 60° because it travels farther
Both reach the same height because they have the same speed
The ball at 60° because the angle gives it lift

quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum height depends on vertical velocity: H = v_y²/(2g). Ball 1: v_y = 30, H = 900/20 = 45 m. Ball 2: v_y = 30sin60° ≈ 26, H ≈ 676/20 ≈ 33.8 m. The vertical throw wins.

quiz_questions.question_text: A package is dropped from a plane flying horizontally at 100 m/s at an altitude of 500 m. Describe the path of the package as seen from the ground.

options.text: The package follows a parabolic trajectory, moving forward at 100 m/s horizontally while accelerating downward at g — it lands far ahead of the drop point
The package falls straight down directly below the drop point
The package curves backward due to air
The package follows a circular path

quiz_questions.explanation: The package retains the plane's horizontal velocity (100 m/s) and gains vertical velocity from gravity. The combination of constant horizontal v and accelerating vertical v produces a parabolic trajectory.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two projectiles are launched at the same speed but at angles of 25° and 65°. How do their ranges compare on level ground?

options.text: They have the same range — sin(2 × 25°) = sin(50°) and sin(2 × 65°) = sin(130°) = sin(50°), so R is the same
The 65° projectile goes farther
The 25° projectile goes farther
Cannot determine without knowing the speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Complementary angles (θ and 90° − θ) give the same range because sin(2θ) = sin(2(90° − θ)) = sin(180° − 2θ) = sin(2θ). So 25° and 65° have identical ranges.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut on the Moon (g = 1.6 m/s²) drops a hammer from 2 m. How long does it take to hit the lunar surface compared to Earth (g = 9.8 m/s²)?

options.text: About 2.5 times longer on the Moon — t_Moon = √(2×2/1.6) ≈ 1.58 s vs t_Earth = √(2×2/9.8) ≈ 0.64 s
The same time on both
About 6 times longer on the Moon
Shorter on the Moon because it's lighter

quiz_questions.explanation: t = √(2h/g). Moon: √(4/1.6) = √2.5 ≈ 1.58 s. Earth: √(4/9.8) ≈ 0.64 s. Ratio: 1.58/0.64 ≈ 2.47, so about 2.5 times longer on the Moon.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soccer ball is kicked at 20 m/s at 45° on level ground. What is the maximum height and total time of flight? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: H = 20 m, T = 4 s
H = 5 m, T = 2 s
H = 10 m, T = 2 s

quiz_questions.question_text: A firefighter aims a hose horizontally from the top of a 20 m building. The water exits at 10 m/s. How far from the base does the water land? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 20 m — fall time: t = √(2 × 20/10) = 2 s; horizontal distance = 10 × 2 = 20 m
5 m

quiz_questions.explanation: t = √(2h/g) = √(40/10) = 2 s. Horizontal distance = vₓ × t = 10 × 2 = 20 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a physics lab, a student launches a marble at 5 m/s at 60° above horizontal from a table 1.2 m high. The marble lands on the floor. Will the range formula R = u²sin(2θ)/g give the correct landing distance?

options.text: No — the range formula only applies when launch and landing heights are the same. Since the marble launches from a table and lands on the floor, the full kinematic equations must be used
Yes — the range formula always works
Yes — just add the table height to the range
No — the range formula only works at 45°

quiz_questions.explanation: R = u²sin(2θ)/g assumes the projectile lands at the same height it was launched. Since the table is 1.2 m above the floor, the landing height differs from the launch height, requiring the complete projectile motion equations.
